REV. EDWARD MAC COY.

Some time after S. ua C.’s inquiry regarding Rev. I. Mac Coy ap-
peared In Vol. III., p. 63, a copy of tha ‘““ Revue Celtique,” Tome 1.,
came into my possession, in which I found his name and address
amongst the subsceribens. Througn the help of the well-known Irish
littérateur, Mr. Richard J. Kelly, B.L., 1 got into communication with
two clergymen, who had known Father Mac Coy, Monsignor Fahey,
D.D., V.G., Gort., and Kather Egan, P.P., Duniry, and by the aid of
the latter, with IFather Mac Coy’s 'FH-I'!li‘l}_T. without wnose assistance I
could never have beon able to glean even the following particulars, for
~ Father Mac Coy appears to have been a man of a singularly retiring dis-
position, a trait which might easily be imferred from his photograph.

Edward Coy, or Mac Coy, as ne wrote his name in later years, was
{ a native of Co. Galway, where he was born on the 5th October, 1839,
T .:* in his father’s residence at Aille, about three miles from the town of
- Loughrea. He was the son of Jonn Coy and Anne Conway, his wife,
| both of whose families were of the well-to-do landholding class.

Young Mac Coy was sent to one of tne schools established under

"(.-;fh

B

f ~ the mnew system of National Education at Leitrim, in the neighbour-
hood of his home, and subsequently he proceeded to a Classical Semin-
~ ary at Loughrea, preparatory to entering Maynooth College.  ILiven
tvhu& early in: nis carcer he gave promise of the great industry and dis-
s metion which he afterwards attained as a sdnolar in Maynooth; 1in
" f""hl&h latter institution he was looked on both by the professors ‘and
‘ ;" - his fellow students as above the ordinary type, and in tho class oxam-

consumption, carried him off after a few montns’ 1llness on the Ind
December, 1872, at the age of 33 years. He was buried with his kindyeg
i Kilnadeema, the churchyard of his native parish.

During IFather Mac Coy’s early mannood, Dr. Mac Hale, Arg,.
bishop of Tuam, Canon Ulick Bourke, and others, were e.-lldenmuring
with voice and pen to keep the Irsh language from falling into disuge,
and catching up their enthusiasm Ifather Mac Coy entered zealously
into tone movement ‘‘ actuated” as he says in the preface to his vl
ume, Danta 1ol-chumaisgthe, *“ by a desire to contribute to the study
of the Gaedhlic and its preservation amongst the people, I have adepted
these selections as the best calculated to exhibit and perpetuate tae
fascinating graces cf this venerable language.” These translations
ifrom Byron, Moore, Davis, ete., into lrish verse he published under
tne above title in 1869 (12mo., pp. xi. + 206), and on its appearance
1t recewved favourable notice from the Irish press and also from M.
Henri Gaidoz in the ‘‘ Revue Celtique,”” Tome 1. But the times were
out of joint, and the hook fell flat. After his death the unsold copies
came into possession of Mr. Patrick Traynor, a Dublin bookseller, who
printed a new title page, with his name as publisher, and re-issued
them in 1878,

At the end of each poem IFather Mac Coy gave a list of Irish words
witan their meanings, and in the early days of the Gaelic League move-
ment, this vocabulary was an acquisition to many of those who were
triying to acquire a knowledge of Irish at a time when Irish dictionaries
were few and only to he obtained at an excessively high price. As to the
merits of the volume, the following remarks of Monsigner Fahey, D.D.,
the distinguished historian of the Diocese of Kilmacduagh, may be of
interest. ‘‘ Father Mac Coy was an accomplished Irish scholar, and
publisned a little volume of Irish poems. Amongst them were tho
translations of some of the finest songs in the Spirit of the Nation,’
but they were a bold rendering of the spirit of the pieco rather than &
atrict translation.”’

Dablin: | OWEN O’BYRNE.

FRISEH. BOOK LOVERS.
By STEPHEN GWYNN, M.P.

Irish people are very odd about books. They are, and overy Irish
writer knows it to his cost, the Teast hook bu ying of publics. Weo, who
write of Ireland, labour like the peasant proprictor dn his hayfield,
under a wholesome cloudy sky, carning our diet of potatoes and butter

THE IRISH BOOK LOVER. 133

qilk, though unhappily often in places where buttermalk, at least, is
yohow to be como by ; and we have always leisure since the day 1s long
jrom dawn to sunset, for passing the time of day with our neighhours.
Qur occupation is eflcetually shielded from theo glare and glamour of
commercialism, and, no doubt, «o much the better for our virtue, Mr.
Yeats has dore more than any man living, perhaps than any man liv-
ing or dead, to raise the fame of Ireland in the craft of letters; but
heaven help Mr. Yeats—heaven help any of us—if existence depended
on the sale of books to the Irisn publie. Yet Ireland is a country of

Llovers: the man for whom books are a passion and a treasure is
perhaps commoner there than anywhere in the world. Lot mo recall
some of tae Irish boek-lovers I have known,

A blind old man living in the steep street of a Norman-Irish town—

in an old borough withh memories of Gael and Gall, planter and sup-
planted, serf and master, monk and Cromwellian, ‘‘ Croppy’”’ and
“Yeo,” memories that he had studied, traditions that he had collected,
all through a long life; and there beside him in tne little sanded kitchen
were stacked the volumes of his most cherished possession—all the
proceedings of tne Irish Parliament. Yet his days were unhappy be-
cause a careless weneration disrespected his books; because his gather-
ed hoard was scattered by the children; because even the great vol-
umes, richly bound, were—so he feared, and not without reason—torn
and abused. I was to send him a hook of my editing, dealing with the
scenes he knew best; he thanked me, but was half inclined to weep,
forecasting tne difficultics he would have in preventing its loss in the
intervals hetween those hours when one or another might read it aloud
to him. He has gone to his rest now, and may his appremticesnip as
clerk to the local poor law board have qualified him for some pleasant
task on that section of the Recording Angel’s staff which deals with: the
deeds done and suffered in Treland.  Another figure rises in my mind,
far away west in Connemara, in the ‘‘next parish to America ”’—a
vetired schoolmastor having 2 name ancient in that region as the dawn
of 5'1&%01*31, and fitly therefore a master of the Gaelic tongue and 1its
printed records: an authority to whom scholars all over lurope rve-
ferred on points of grammar, of usage, even of detailed historic fact.
His tiny shelves were laden with volumes of price, gifts, many of them
inscribed with illustrions names, and yeb standing Dy others waich
st have heen purchased at a cost that might well have seemed be-
Jond the means of any who dwelt in Udat remote cotitago; and all wero

134 THE IRISH BOOK LOVIR.

cuarded liko the apples of the Hesperidos. Yot for those in whom the
cuardian discerned a genuine lovo of knowledge, no dragon had to be
arcumvented : books and his skill in tnem were alike freely at com.
mand.

Richer in books, not less nidh in courtesy and scholar’s generosity,
was one whose less left us all impoverished—Ceesar Litton Falkiner, 5
true Irish bibliophile, but happily endowed witn what too many of our
erudite have lacked. He had the lucidus ordo, the faculty for sifting
and arranging, for seleoting the relevant fact out of his store; he was
a good writer as well as a good reader.  Often our best beok-lovers
have produced some magnum opus whose bulk 1s crammed with a hotch-
potch where saape or outline is none; and a gubject like the ‘‘ History
of the Irish Brigade,” fit to provide a hundred romances, becomes
merely an impenetrable thicket of intertangled detail. Some such de-
fect 1n architectonic quality, some sucn lack of binding power, robbed
the world of what another Irishman might have given to it. Those of
us wno knew W. J. Craig knew him as a passionate devourer of books,
yet least of all men a bookworm. He had drawn the very essence out
of all Elizabethan and Jacobean literature. For half a lifetime he lab-
oured, with toil like a miner’s, to bring into the light a glossary of tae
Ehzabethan English ; yet for want of the simplest, almost the mechani-
cal, aptitude of a writer’s craft ho never accomplished anything. These
are a few names at random.—Special to ‘“ Freeman's Journal,”’ 25th
January, 1913.

A CHAT ON CATALOGULS.

Messrs. Hanma & Neale have issued in No. 10 a very 'mft-e-resting
catalogue of books at reasonable prices. Amongst some of the raritie
we note Gimlette’s Huguenot Scttlers, £2; Madden’s United Irishmey
4 vols., £4; a large collection cf old Irish newspaper cuttings made by
Dr. Madden, a scrapbook containing MS. poems by himself and other

members of his family, in 3 vols., £2, and a volume of - his poems of E
T

o -

whic only 20 copies were printed at Havana, 10s.; O'Reilly’s Irish
Writers, £2 2s.; Weblb’s Compendium, £3; Hardiman’s Galway, £6;
Reeve’s Down and Connor, £2 2s.; two scarce periodicals, The Protest-
ant Confederate, 1836, 8s. 6d., and The Ballitore Magazine, 2s. 6d.;
Jim Blake’s Tour from Clonave to Londen, with sketches by I&. N. and
notes by A. A., 5s. There is an interesting note in the ** Atheneum”
for lst February regarding these two, whnich may be quoted here:
“« Erskine Nicol had, in early life, a valuable Scotch patron, the late
Andrew Armstrong, who was settled in Dublin, with a lodge in Conne-
mara, and this excellent man, himself the centre of a most witty
society, was tne cause of Nicol knowing and understanding the Irish

peasant as he then existed.”

There is a fine array of first editions of Lever’s mnovels which, if
somewhat out of fashion now as literature, will one day be valuable for
the plates if for nothing else. Maurice and Bergetta, or tne Priest of
Rahery, is cheap at 2s. It was written by Wm. Parnell, sometime M.D.
There are long lists of the works of Synrge and Yeats at prices to suib
all pockets, and quite a dozen of Mr. Dix’s valuable Lists of Local Irish
Printing, some of which have already appeared in our own pages.

In a recent catalogue of autographs issued by Messrs. Maggs, of
the Strand, is included a letter of Sir William Napier, the historian

of the Peninsular War, in which he complains that ¢ Maxwell in his

Life of Wollington has taken from my work, without acknowtedgment,
ag many passages as gives eleven thousand words. Mr. Lever in l{iﬁ
Charles O’Malley has stolen whole passages and descriptions.’

Tone Georgo T. Juckes Co., 35, St. Mactin’s Court, W.C., rooently
cabalogued a romarkable collection of 189 autograph letters of cminent
literary mon and women addressed to Sir Willinm and Lady Wilde,
hatwoon the years 1860 and 1890. A mero glance at the names of tho
Wx'ilt-ers will bo intercating, Wmn, A.Plni:ni;_ijllalm , Patrick Bordan, James
Bourke, editor of the ‘*Shamrock,” Smith 0’Brien, Camon Bourke,
Crofton Crolker, Wm. Carleton, wio 1m a pathotic passago writes, For

V'

God’s sake, come up to me, your old friend is dying.”” There were no
Jess than ten from Gavan Duffy, some dating from before the mar-
rage of the recipient, and five from W. J. Fitzpatrick, one of eleven
PAES datad 1856, one from IEdward Hayes, who compiled the ‘¢ Bal-
lads of Ireland,” from Mr. and Mrs. S. C. Hall and J. K. Ingram,
p. . MacQarthy, May Laffan, Rosa Mulholland, tnree from Dr.
Madden, John Boyle O’Raelly, P. J. Smyth, T. D. Sullivan, Meadows
Taylor, and Emerscn Tennant. This lot was priced at £€12. A second
lot of 106, chiefly from English literary folk amd of later date, was
priced at £5, whilst a tnird, consisting of 32 documents, including
letters from Chas. Maturin, of New York, Martin Haverty, two letters
and two poems from John Trancis Waller, and others, sold for 23s.

Needless to add, they were all ‘‘ snapped up’ by cute Americans.

IRISH LITERARY SOCLETY.

Mrs. J. R. Green delivered an address on ‘‘ How Henry VIIL, Bo-

came King of Ireland ’’ on Saturday, the 8th Iebruary. Professor J.
H. Morgan presided, in the absence, through illness, of Lord Mac-
Donnell. Mrs. Green spoke witin all her accustomed charm of style

and mastery of dooumentary evidence, and for some three-quarters

of an hour held a crowded audience in rapt attention. It was a sorry

story she had to tell of the blandishments by which tne Tudor despot
persuaded an Irish Parliament to pass a declaratory Aot recognising
his title to the kingship, which, as he was quick to sce, was, sO far
as it rested on a Papal grant to the Angevin kings, imperilled by the
repudiation of Papal authority in these islands. The Parliament was
carefully packed by new creataons in tho Upper House and by the
election of non-resident members for the Lower, while the semblance

sentation was achieved by securing the attendance

of a natiomal repre

Mrs. Green, 1 her reply to some friendly criticism by Mr. Philip
Wilson, who moved the vote of thanks, made one or two very striking
and suggestive remarks as to aspersions on that Irish civilisation and
Irish loyalty which she had dome so much to indicate. Challenged on
the subject of the betrayal of the Irish cause in Tudor times by the
cadets of Irish families, she said she had gone through each of suca
cases, and had found that in every instance the traitor had been * fos-
tered ’ in England, and was, 1n fact, an Irishman whese childhood had
been corrupted. As to Irish civilisavion, she added that she had lately
been studying the architecture on the western coast of Ireland and
along the banks of the Shannon, and had been struck by tne fact that
this part of ‘the country was as rich in good examples of Continental
etyles of the pre-Tudor period as the Englisn ‘‘ Pale’ was poor—a
fact which went far to refute the idea that Ireland beyond the Pale was
steeped in barbarism. -

One of the pledsantest and raciest of the Tea Table Talks of the
session was given by Mrs. Milligan-Fox, author of the ‘‘Annals of the
Irish Harpers,”” on Wednesday, 12th February, in which she detailed
ber experionces when * Folk-Song hunting in Co. Waterford ”’ lasb
eummer. The speaker, whose fame as an authority on Irish music 18
world-wude, 1s also an indefatigable ocolleotor of the folk-songs of Ire-
land, and spends a portion’ of ewery year in this pleasant pursuit.
BShe has utilized the services of the phonograph for their: preservation

and . reproduction, and great-was the wonderment, she told us, of the.

peasants m the Comeragh mountains at hearing their songs both in
Gaelic. and Emglish repeated on the instrument. Father Power, of
Portlaw, always to the fore in rondering assistanoe, passed the word,
and a whole group of thé ancient singers of his flock assembled and
samg traditional airs. The Marchioness of Waterford, hergelf a fin-
whed musician, also lent ready nid, - whilst Robert: Weldon, tho last
of tho Gaolic bards, sang many songs of 'his dgwm composition, one, an
extomporised ‘‘ Welcome.””  All thése wero reproduced for the edifi-
cation of the audienoco, interspersed ‘with ‘many o witty story and in-

children’s political apostacy’”; and concludes with his well-worn

Toe QUEsTION OF IrisE NartroNavity. By Stephen J. Brown, S.J.
(Sealey, Bryers.) This—a reprint from * Studies’—is pernaps the
best considered, the most accurate, and the most carefully compiled
pamphlet regarding Irish affairs generally that has appeared recently.
Its jorty-four pages are soaked and saturated with information and
reforences culled with infinite care and exhaustless patience from all
sources. The compiler of ‘“ A Guide to Books on Ireland,” has not
spent his time in vain. It is a book wwitten from the heart of a true
Inshman, with a pen of almost painful accuracy of detail and substan-
tiation, and, as such, must make its mark. The writer is one of the
deepest, thinkers and hardest workers for Irish Ireland. We expect
great thimgs from him in the years to come, judging by his present
initial literary effort. A.

written, ““The Spanish Brothers’’ was published 1n the !‘ Family
Treasury » in 1870. In book form it attained great populamity, and was

translated into many languages. ‘‘ Miss Aloock was a woman of rare.

Wexford.

* ¥ Hea was not. Wm. Carleton, junr., was never, to our know-

We have the pleasure of exclusively announcing that the long-de-
sired ‘‘ Life of Isaac Butt’’—“ The Father of Home Rule’’—ig to
appear at last. It is being written, with the full consent and co-oper-
ation of Miss Rosa Butt, the surviving daughter of the brmilliant advo-
cate amd statesman, by Mr. IFrank MacDonagh, the London editor of
““ The Northern Whig,”” and a valued contributor to our own pages.

The two series of articles contributed to ‘‘ The Times’ by the late
W. . Monypenny in tne earlier months of last year, on ‘ Ireland and 3?
Home Rule,”” and ‘‘ The Present Home Rule Bill,”’ will shortly be re-
1ssued as a bock by Mr. Murray under tho title, ‘“The Two Irish
Nations: An Essay on Home Rule.” ‘

Mr. Worner Laurie wall publish during the spring ‘“ A Gentleman
of Ireland, being the Life of George Henry Moore, Sportsmam, Travel- '
ler, and Politician,” by his son Colonel Maurice George Moore, C.B.. _
a brotnzr of Mr. Gecrge Moore, the novelist. The subject of the
memoir, after travellng through the Caucasus shorftly after the Rus- |
gran occupation, as well as through Persia, Syria, and Egypt, returned
home wowards the middle of last century to become one of the best
31300.1)19(:}13&0 riders in Ireland; but, during the Irish famine, sold his i

horses and devoted himsolf to the relief of the people. Later he enter-
ed Parliament, organized the Irish memberns into a party of ¢ Inde- i
pendent, Opposition,” fought more than ome political duel, returned i
for a timg to racing, and, re-entering Parliament in 1868, helped to |
start the Homo Rule movement., , |
